main_ingredients: fish collar (kama — yellowtail, salmon, or mackerel)
miso soup (miso paste, dashi stock, tofu, wakame seaweed)
allergens: gluten (miso, soy sauce, tempura batter if included)

serving.description: 1 set meal (grilled fish collar, steamed rice, miso soup, side dishes)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal serving of approximately 550 g, comprising a salt-grilled fish collar, a bowl of steamed white rice, miso soup, and one or two small side dishes such as pickled vegetables or a small salad.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat; Rice, white, cooked; Soup, miso; composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA or HPB entry exists for a complete shioyaki kama set meal. Per-100g values were derived by weighted composite estimation using USDA FoodData Central entries: (1) Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175168) for the grilled fish collar component; (2) Rice, white, long-grain, cooked (FDC ID 169756) for the rice component; (3) Soup, miso, prepared (FDC ID 172462) for the miso soup component; and (4) Vegetables, mixed, cooked (FDC ID 170416) for side dish components. Approximate component weight proportions used: fish collar 30%, rice 35%, miso soup 25%, sides 10%. Input record values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite and retained where consistent. Sodium value reflects salt-grilling and miso paste contribution and is consistent with the composite estimate. All values are estimates; exact restaurant preparation may vary.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for a shioyaki kama set meal. GI estimated at 54 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white rice (GI ~64–72 per University of Sydney GI Database, entry for white rice, boiled). The overall meal GI is moderated downward to approximately 54 by the substantial protein (fish collar ~8.2g/100g), fat (5.1g/100g), and dietary fibre (0.7g/100g) content of the set, which slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (54 × 55g carbohydrate in 550g serving) / 100 = 29.7. Carbohydrate per serving estimated as 10g/100g × 550g = 55g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from salt-grilling, miso paste, soy sauce, and dashi stock
hypertension.guidance: At 272.7 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,500 mg per 550 g serving — over 65% of the 2,300 mg daily limit), this set meal poses a significant sodium burden for hypertensive individuals; request minimal salt application during grilling, reduced miso paste and soy sauce, and avoid adding table soy sauce.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load from white rice with low glycemic index; protein and fat moderate glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 29.7 per serving is moderate-to-high for type 2 diabetes management; individuals should request a smaller rice portion or substitute brown rice, avoid tempura if included, and pair the meal with the fish and miso soup first to blunt the postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from fatty fish collar offset by elevated sodium from shioyaki preparation and miso
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fish collar (yellowtail, salmon, or mackerel) provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) and saturated fat is low at 1.1 g/100 g, which is favourable; however, the high sodium content (~1,500 mg per serving) is a significant cardiovascular risk factor and individuals with CVD should request reduced-sodium preparation and limit portion size.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids from fish collar beneficial; sodium from miso and soy sauce may worsen tissue swelling
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Fatty fish collar is rich in EPA/DHA omega-3 fatty acids that directly reduce joint inflammation, making this a beneficial protein source for rheumatoid arthritis; however, the high sodium content from miso paste and salt-grilling may exacerbate RA-related tissue swelling and should be minimised by requesting reduced-sodium preparation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shioyaki Grilled Fish Collar Set Meal (塩焼きカマ定食, Shioyaki Kama Teishoku; Chinese: 盐烤鱼颈肉套餐; Malay: Set Makan Ikan Panggang Garam) is a traditional Japanese set meal (teishoku) served at Japanese restaurants in Singapore and Japan. The centrepiece is a fish collar (kama) — the fatty, flavourful section of the fish just behind the head and gills — prepared using the shioyaki technique: lightly salted and grilled over high heat until the skin is crisp and the flesh is moist and tender. Common fish used include yellowtail (hamachi), salmon, or mackerel. The set is typically accompanied by a bowl of steamed white rice, a bowl of miso soup made with dashi stock, tofu, and wakame seaweed, and one or two small side dishes such as pickled vegetables (tsukemono) or a small salad. Some versions include tempura as an additional side. The fish collar is prized for its high fat content, yielding a rich, succulent texture with a lightly charred, savoury exterior from the salt-grilling. Nutritionally, the set provides approximately 118 kcal per 100 g, with 8.2 g protein, 5.1 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from the fatty fish), and 10 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Sodium is elevated at 272.7 mg/100 g due to the salt-grilling process, miso paste, and soy sauce. The meal is a good source of omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA) from the fatty fish collar, which are cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory. Clinically, the high sodium content is the primary concern for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients, while the moderate glycaemic load from white rice requires portion management for type 2 diabetes. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with fish, soy, or gluten allergies.

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for a complete shioyaki kama set meal. Per-100g values were derived by weighted composite estimation from USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled fish, cooked white rice, miso soup, and mixed vegetables. Input record values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this composite and are broadly consistent. GI is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI moderated by protein and fat content. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and variability in restaurant preparation.
notes: 1. Per-100g nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central entries for the component foods (grilled fish, white rice, miso soup, mixed vegetables) weighted by approximate portion proportions (fish collar 30%, rice 35%, miso soup 25%, sides 10%). Exact restaurant preparation will vary. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite Japanese set meal are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; individual component data exists but reliable composite values cannot be confirmed without laboratory analysis. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.04 g/100g from the input record; this trace value is plausible for a grilled fish and rice set meal and is retained. 4. The input record listed three portion entries with duplicate and inconsistent weights (550g x 2, 650g); the canonical serving size of 550g has been adopted as the default, representing a standard Japanese restaurant set meal. 5. Halal status is marked false as miso paste and soy sauce may contain alcohol-based fermentation products and the restaurant's halal certification status is unknown. 6. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for shioyaki kama set meal was found in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of 2024. 7. This dish is served in Singapore at a Japanese restaurant; multilingual local names have been provided in Chinese (盐烤鱼颈肉套餐 / 鹽烤魚頸肉套餐), Malay (Set Makan Ikan Panggang Garam), Tamil (உப்பு வறுத்த மீன் காலர் தொகுப்பு உணவு), and Hindi (नमक-भुना मछली कॉलर सेट भोजन) to satisfy G6 multilingual requirements for Singapore-served dishes, in addition to the mandatory Japanese name (塩焼きカマ定食).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Drafted full RESULT JSON. Replaced forbidden source (foodo.sg) with USDA FoodData Central composite estimation. Corrected food_name to canonical form. Added Japanese local name. Added GI estimate. Added all three required health_context conditions. Documented all -1 fields in notes. Verified aliases consistency.
Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent'. Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' for GI 54.

Iteration 3: Added mandatory multilingual local names for Singapore-served dish context: Chinese simplified (盐烤鱼颈肉套餐), Chinese traditional (鹽烤魚頸肉套餐), Malay (Set Makan Ikan Panggang Garam), Tamil (உப்பு வறுத்த மீன் காலர் தொகுப்பு உணவு), Hindi (नमक-भुना मछली कॉलर सेट भोजन). Added all new local-language names to flat aliases array and aliases_by_language to satisfy G6 and Q8. Updated notes to document multilingual additions.
